food_subgroup: Deep-fried taro (yam) pastry shell with savoury pork and dried shrimp filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Wu Gok. Closest parent is a deep-fried starchy pastry with savoury meat filling. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried taro pastry shell with savoury pork and dried shrimp filling (Cantonese wu gok dim sum style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CEB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for deep-fried starchy pastry; no exact wu gok code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept exists for wu gok or deep-fried taro puff dim sum.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard wu gok piece weighs approximately 60g and is typically served as part of a dim sum spread at yum cha.

nutrient_source.title: Wu Gok (Deep-fried Taro Puff) — HPB Singapore Foodo; estimated from components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for wu gok / deep-fried taro puff, supplemented by component-level estimation for micronutrients not directly reported. All values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central taro and pork entries for plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Taro (Colocasia esculenta) — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to wu gok
gi_source.source_match_notes: Taro has a documented GI of approximately 55–65 in the University of Sydney GI Database. A midpoint value of GI 58 is applied to wu gok. The high lard content and deep-frying fat in the pastry shell significantly suppress the glycaemic response relative to plain taro. GL per 60g serving = (58 × 14.7g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 8.5, classified as moderate GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 420 mg per 100g from oyster sauce, dried shrimp, and soy sauce in the filling
hypertension.guidance: One 60g piece delivers approximately 252 mg sodium; individuals managing hypertension should limit intake to one piece per sitting and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dim sum items such as siu mai or cheung fun with soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load (GL 8.5 per 60g piece) despite medium GI taro base, attenuated by high fat content from lard and deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: One 60g piece provides 14.7g available carbohydrate with a GL of 8.5, which is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece per meal and account for the high fat content (10.1g per piece) which contributes to total energy density of 167 kcal per piece.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (16.8g/100g) including saturated fat (5.2g/100g) from lard in the pastry and deep-frying oil absorption, plus trans fat (0.1g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wu gok is one of the most energy-dense dim sum items due to lard in the pastry and deep-frying oil absorption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional indulgence, as one 60g piece delivers 3.1g saturated fat and 10.1g total fat, contributing significantly to daily saturated fat limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wu Gok (芋角), also known as deep-fried taro puff or woo kok, is one of the most technically demanding and visually spectacular items in Cantonese dim sum cuisine. The dish consists of a filling of savoury minced pork, dried shrimp, and mushroom seasoned with oyster sauce, enclosed in a shell made from mashed taro (Chinese taro/yam, Colocasia esculenta) mixed with wheat starch and lard. When deep-fried in very hot oil, the shell puffs up and forms a characteristic lacy, honeycomb-like exterior — a result of steam escaping through the taro-starch-lard matrix. The result is an extraordinary textural contrast: a crispy, airy, almost transparent outer lattice surrounding a dense, flavourful savoury filling. Wu gok must be eaten immediately after frying as the crispy shell softens within minutes. Nutritionally, wu gok is one of the most energy-dense dim sum items at 278 kcal per 100g, driven by lard in the pastry and deep-frying oil absorption. A standard 60g piece delivers approximately 167 kcal, 5.7g protein, 10.1g fat (3.1g saturated), and 14.7g carbohydrate with a moderate glycaemic load of 8.5. The taro base contributes potassium (388 mg/100g), dietary fibre (2.5g/100g), and vitamin B6 (0.28 mg/100g). Sodium is moderate at 420 mg/100g, primarily from the savoury filling ingredients. Wu gok is a premium dim sum delicacy originating from Guangdong (Canton), China, and is a staple of yum cha (dim sum brunch) in Cantonese restaurants across Singapore, Hong Kong, and the Chinese diaspora worldwide.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for wu gok, supplemented by component-level estimation for micronutrients. The HPB Foodo entry is an adapted/estimated composite rather than a direct laboratory analysis of a single standardised recipe. GI is an analogous estimate based on taro GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for wu gok (deep-fried taro puff with pork filling) is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite deep-fried dim sum item is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. All other micronutrient values are estimated from component-level data (taro, pork, dried shrimp) cross-referenced against HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on taro GI range (55–65) from the University of Sydney GI Database; the high lard and deep-frying fat content attenuates the glycaemic response. trans_fat_g of 0.1g/100g reflects trace levels from deep-frying oil; this is a plausible estimate consistent with HPB Foodo data for similar deep-fried dim sum items.
